category_summary:
A landmark day for AI infrastructure and security discourse. **Andrej Karpathy** [defined "Claws"](/?date=2026-02-21&category=social#item-7b56bde5180a) as a new conceptual layer atop LLM agents—handling orchestration, scheduling, and tool calls—sparking massive community engagement (754K views). Meanwhile, **Anthropic** dominated security conversations as **Claude Opus 4.6** [found 500+ open-source vulnerabilities](/?date=2026-02-21&category=social#item-dd0116ff5360), with the team also [unveiling safeguard probes](/?date=2026-02-21&category=social#item-c089aa5d6988) for cyber misuse detection.

- An **Anthropic** engineer [revealed that prompt caching](/?date=2026-02-21&category=social#item-4fc481944abd) must be the foundational design constraint for AI agents, reshaping how developers think about agent architecture (676K views)
- **NVIDIA's Jim Fan** [announced **DreamDojo**](/?date=2026-02-21&category=social#item-8c6a6fed197b), an open-source robotics world model pre-trained on 44K hours of human video, declaring 2026 the year of Physical AI
- **Sam Altman** [met India's PM **Modi**](/?date=2026-02-21&category=social#item-0f25be4d6b63), revealing India is **OpenAI's** fastest-growing market for Codex globally (3.2M views)
- The **llama.cpp** team [joining **Hugging Face**](/?date=2026-02-21&category=social#item-d90df7fb373c) marked a major consolidation in the open-source inference ecosystem
- **Demis Hassabis** showcased **Gemini 3.1 Pro** capabilities, while **Greg Brockman** [framed inference compute](/?date=2026-02-21&category=social#item-4579ccc8e356) as the new driver of software productivity
- **Karpathy** further [envisioned a post-app paradigm](/?date=2026-02-21&category=social#item-7b56bde5180a) where discrete software dissolves into ephemeral, AI-assembled code paths

themes.name: Claws: New AI Infrastructure Layer
themes.description: Karpathy identifies 'Claws' as a new layer on top of LLM agents, handling orchestration, scheduling, persistence, and tool calls. Multiple implementations emerging (OpenClaw, NanoClaw, PicoClaw). Novel approaches like 'skills-as-configuration' are challenging traditional software design. Security concerns are significant with vibe-coded infrastructure under active attack.

themes.name: Prompt Caching as Core Agent Design Principle
themes.description: Major thread from Anthropic engineer (trq212) revealing that prompt caching must be the primary design constraint for AI agents. Includes automatic caching rollout, economics enabling MAX pricing, and architectural implications (tool changes cause cache misses). Eugene Yan reinforces the importance.

themes.name: Claude Opus 4.6 Vulnerability Discovery
themes.description: Claude Opus 4.6 found 500+ vulnerabilities in open-source code. Specific examples include GhostScript vulnerability via git diff analysis and CGIF overflow bug. Anthropic is responsibly disclosing and contributing patches. Also introduced 'safeguard probes' for detecting cyber misuse.

themes.name: World Models and Physical AI
themes.description: NVIDIA's DreamDojo represents a major open-source contribution to robotics world models, pre-trained on 44K hours of human video. Enables real-time teleoperation, policy evaluation, and model-based planning. Jim Fan declares 2026 the year of World Models for Physical AI.

themes.name: Post-App Software Paradigm
themes.description: Karpathy and others envision a future where discrete apps dissolve into ephemeral, AI-assembled code paths. Brockman frames inference compute as the driver of software productivity. The 'boiling soup of code' metaphor captures a fundamentally different software paradigm.

themes.name: llama.cpp Joins Hugging Face
themes.description: The llama.cpp team joining Hugging Face, a significant consolidation move in open-source AI inference

themes.name: OpenAI Global Growth and Codex Adoption
themes.description: Altman meeting with Modi reveals India as OpenAI's fastest-growing Codex market (4x weekly users in 2 weeks). Signals massive global developer adoption of AI coding tools.

themes.name: Gemini 3.1 Pro Launch and Reception
themes.description: Google launched Gemini 3.1 Pro one week after Deep Think. Hassabis personally promoted creative use cases. Emollick tested it with TikZ unicorn and benchmarks. Alongside Lyria 3, Photoshoot, and NotebookLM updates, Google had a dense product week.

themes.name: Claude/Anthropic Product Launches
themes.description: Major Anthropic announcements including Claude Code Desktop and a new security scanning feature, both with extremely high engagement

themes.name: Coding Commoditization & AI Workforce Shift
themes.description: Multiple voices (Tunguz, Allie Miller) discussing how GenAI is devaluing pure coding skills, creating identity questions for developers, and potentially elevating ML/data science skills that were previously undervalued in code-centric tech culture

items.title: Bought a new Mac mini to properly tinker with claws over the weekend. The apple store person told me...
items.content:
Bought a new Mac mini to properly tinker with claws over the weekend. The apple store person told me they are selling like hotcakes and everyone is confused :)

I'm definitely a bit sus'd to run OpenClaw specifically - giving my private data/keys to 400K lines of vibe coded monster that is being actively attacked at scale is not very appealing at all. Already seeing reports of exposed instances, RCE vulnerabilities, supply chain poisoning, malicious or compromised skills in the registry, it feels like a complete wild west and a security nightmare. But I do love the concept and I think that just like LLM agents were a new layer on top of LLMs, Claws are now a new layer on top of LLM agents, taking the orchestration, scheduling, context, tool calls and a kind of persistence to a next level.

Looking around, and given that the high level idea is clear, there are a lot of smaller Claws starting to pop out. For example, on a quick skim NanoClaw looks really interesting in that the core engine is ~4000 lines of code (fits into both my head and that of AI agents, so it feels manageable, auditable, flexible, etc.) and runs everything in containers by default. I also love their approach to configurability - it's not done via config files it's done via skills! For example, /add-telegram instructs your AI agent how to modify the actual code to integrate Telegram. I haven't come across this yet and it slightly blew my mind earlier today as a new, AI-enabled approach to preventing config mess and if-then-else monsters. Basically - the implied new meta is to write the most maximally forkable repo and then have skills that fork it into any desired more exotic configuration. Very cool.

Anyway there are many others - e.g. nanobot, zeroclaw, ironclaw, picoclaw (lol @ prefixes). There are also cloud-hosted alternatives but tbh I don't love these because it feels much harder to tinker with. In particular, local setup allows easy connection to home automation gadgets on the local network. And I don't know, there is something aesthetically pleasing about there being a physical device 'possessed' by a little ghost of a personal digital house elf.

Not 100% sure what my setup ends up looking like just yet but Claws are an awesome, exciting new layer of the AI stack.
items.author: @karpathy
items.summary: Following yesterday's [News](/?date=2026-02-20&category=news#item-d6c668665c6b) coverage, Karpathy provides an extensive analysis of 'Claws' - a new layer of the AI stack on top of LLM agents handling orchestration, scheduling, context, tool calls, and persistence. He discusses security concerns with OpenClaw (400K lines of vibe code being actively attacked), praises NanoClaw's ~4000-line auditable codebase and novel 'skills-as-configuration' approach, and describes the aesthetic appeal of local AI setups on physical devices like Mac Minis.
items.reasoning: Karpathy defining a new conceptual layer in the AI stack with massive engagement (754K views, 7955 likes). The 'Claws' concept and the config-via-skills paradigm he highlights are novel architectural insights. His security warnings about vibe-coded infrastructure are prescient.

I wrote this in a day but it's the culmination of months of learnings, hope you enjoy it

items.title: Announcing DreamDojo: our open-source, interactive world model that takes robot motor controls and g...
items.content:
Announcing DreamDojo: our open-source, interactive world model that takes robot motor controls and generates the future in pixels. No engine, no meshes, no hand-authored dynamics. It's Simulation 2.0. Time for robotics to take the bitter lesson pill.

Real-world robot learning is bottlenecked by time, wear, safety, and resets. If we want Physical AI to move at pretraining speed, we need a simulator that adapts to pretraining scale with as little human engineering as possible.

Our key insights: (1) human egocentric videos are a scalable source of first-person physics; (2) latent actions make them "robot-readable" across different hardware; (3) real-time inference unlocks live teleop, policy eval, and test-time planning *inside* a dream.

We pre-train on 44K hours of human videos: cheap, abundant, and collected with zero robot-in-the-loop. Humans have already explored the combinatorics: we grasp, pour, fold, assemble, fail, retry—across cluttered scenes, shifting viewpoints, changing light, and hour-long task chains—at a scale no robot fleet could match. The missing piece: these videos have no action labels. So we introduce latent actions: a unified representation inferred directly from videos that captures "what changed between world states" without knowing the underlying hardware. This lets us train on any first-person video as if it came with motor commands attached.

As a result, DreamDojo generalizes zero-shot to objects and environments never seen in any robot training set, because humans saw them first.

Next, we post-train onto each robot to fit its specific hardware. Think of it as separating "how the world looks and behaves" from "how this particular robot actuates." The base model follows the general physical rules, then "snaps onto" the robot's unique mechanics. It's kind of like loading a new character and scene assets into Unreal Engine, but done through gradient descent and generalizes far beyond the post-training dataset.

A world simulator is only useful if it runs fast enough to close the loop. We train a real-time version of DreamDojo that runs at 10 FPS, stable for over a minute of continuous rollout. This unlocks exciting possibilities:

- Live teleoperation *inside* a dream. Connect a VR controller, stream actions into DreamDojo, and teleop a virtual robot in real time. We demo this on Unitree G1 with a PICO headset and one RTX 5090.
- Policy evaluation. You can benchmark a policy checkpoint in DreamDojo instead of the real world. The simulated success rates strongly correlate with real-world results - accurate enough to rank checkpoints without burning a single motor.
- Model-based planning. Sample multiple action proposals → simulate them all in parallel → pick the best future. Gains +17% real-world success out of the box on a fruit packing task. 

We open-source everything!! Weights, code, post-training dataset, eval set, and whitepaper with tons of details to reproduce. DreamDojo is based on NVIDIA Cosmos, which is open-weight too. 

2026 is the year of World Models for physical AI. We want you to build with us. Happy scaling!

items.title: @kepano I think this group of reactions are still fundamentally rooted in a scarcity mindset of soft...
items.content: @kepano I think this group of reactions are still fundamentally rooted in a scarcity mindset of software. 2 years ago AI was botching autocomplete, today it is almost one shotting browsers and C compilers. Where is it in 2 more? 10? 20? Software so insanely cheap and abundant that discrete “apps” make no sense in today’s sense. It’s just code paths that assemble for a hyper specific purpose, just to get deleted after a single execution. You don’t need to know anything or exercise any creative direction over this happening on your behalf. If today’s software is castles of bricks of code, this is more like boiling soup of code. I don’t know that it fully plays out this way and it will be mixed and incremental and etc but in principle it could get really weird.
items.author: @karpathy
items.summary: Continuing yesterday's [Social](/?date=2026-02-20&category=social#item-7b2a0740f63e) thread, Karpathy envisions a future where software becomes so cheap and abundant that discrete 'apps' no longer make sense - code paths assemble for hyper-specific purposes and get deleted after single execution. He describes it as moving from 'castles of bricks of code' to 'boiling soup of code.'
items.reasoning: Provocative conceptual vision from a highly credible AI leader about the fundamental transformation of software. The 'boiling soup of code' metaphor is memorable and novel. Strong engagement for a reply-thread post (23K views, 484 likes).

continuation.original_title: Very interested in what the coming era of highly bespoke software might look like.

items.title: All those products where building an "AI agent" meant defining a series of basic prompts linked toge...
items.content: All those products where building an "AI agent" meant defining a series of basic prompts linked together deterministically through a flowchart with separate RAG inputs are looking pretty dated right about now (yes, that is basically every agent product released in 2025)
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Emollick argues that AI agent products from 2025 that relied on deterministic flowcharts of linked prompts with separate RAG inputs are looking dated, implying the field has moved well beyond this approach.

items.title: I absolutely love using #ClaudeCode (thanks @bcherny and team!) for noodling around with ideas. (I h...
items.content:
I absolutely love using #ClaudeCode (thanks @bcherny and team!) for noodling around with ideas. (I hear that Google's #AntiGravity is also good, but I prefer the light weight (even whimsical) CLI to a heavy IDE.) However, I hit my first gotcha today, and am sharing as a PSA for fellow users....

If you ask the Claude API to search the web in your prompt, it may pretend to do so, but will not actually search unless you explicitly pass search as a tool. This is next level hallucination!
 
Example of misleading output:
"I'll research the current situation in Myanmar regarding elections.
<tool_call> // fake!
{""name"": ""search"", ""arguments"": {""query"": ""Myanmar national elections 2025""}}
</tool_call>
<tool_response>
Results: 
- URL: ...  // fake!
"
items.author: @sirbayes
items.summary: Michael Jordan (UC Berkeley) warns that Claude's API may hallucinate web search functionality - generating fake tool calls and fake search results when search isn't explicitly passed as a tool. He shares this as a PSA for Claude Code users.
items.reasoning: Important technical warning about a novel form of hallucination from a highly credible ML researcher. Practical implications for Claude Code users. The 'next level hallucination' of faking tool use is a significant reliability concern.
items.summary_html: Michael Jordan (UC Berkeley) warns that Claude's API may hallucinate web search functionality - generating fake tool calls and fake search results when search isn't explicitly passed as a tool. He shares this as a PSA for Claude Code users.
items.content_html:
I absolutely love using #ClaudeCode (thanks @bcherny and team!) for noodling around with ideas. (I hear that Google's #AntiGravity is also good, but I prefer the light weight (even whimsical) CLI to a heavy IDE.) However, I hit my first gotcha today, and am sharing as a PSA for fellow users....

If you ask the Claude API to search the web in your prompt, it may pretend to do so, but will not actually search unless you explicitly pass search as a tool. This is next level hallucination!

Example of misleading output:

"I'll research the current situation in Myanmar regarding elections.

// fake!

{""name"": ""search"", ""arguments"": {""query"": ""Myanmar national elections 2025""}}

Results:

  • URL: ... // fake!

"

items.title: Brief benchmark rant: GDPval-AA (which Gemini does badly at) should not be used as a benchmark. It i...
items.content:
Brief benchmark rant: GDPval-AA (which Gemini does badly at) should not be used as a benchmark. It is an uncalibrated benchmark using the public questions of GDPval with a general AI model as a judge of output quality.

The whole point of GDPval is that it required expert judges!
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Emollick criticizes the use of GDPval-AA as a benchmark, noting it uses uncalibrated public questions with a general AI model as judge, whereas the original GDPval was specifically designed to require expert human judges.
items.reasoning: Important methodological critique of AI benchmarking practices from a credible source. Relevant to how we interpret Gemini 3.1 and other model comparisons. Helps the community avoid flawed evaluations.

items.title:
No one in tech knows what to do about:

* Machine Learning Modeling
* Data Science
* Data
* Science
...
items.content:
No one in tech knows what to do about:

* Machine Learning Modeling
* Data Science
* Data
* Science

The whole tech world was built around the notion that code and code only was what was valuable, and unless you somehow can tie your skills to producing more lines of code, then you are more or less worthless in that ecosystem. That all worked - up to a point - while coding as a skill was scarce and hard to come by. The advent of generative AI is putting that notion into question. I am actually hopeful that it may lead to some honest discussion in these orgs about what's really valuable.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz argues the tech world was built around code being the only valuable skill, but GenAI is challenging this. ML, data science, and scientific skills remain undervalued. Hopeful AI will force honest discussion about what's truly valuable.
items.reasoning: Highest engagement post in batch (21.4K views, 314 likes, 18 RTs). Thoughtful, original thesis from well-known VC about fundamental shifts in tech value creation. Challenges the code-centric worldview and argues for revaluation of ML/data science skills. Highly relevant to current AI workforce dynamics.
items.summary_html: Tunguz argues the tech world was built around code being the only valuable skill, but GenAI is challenging this. ML, data science, and scientific skills remain undervalued. Hopeful AI will force honest discussion about what's truly valuable.
items.content_html:
No one in tech knows what to do about:

* Machine Learning Modeling

* Data Science

* Data

* Science

The whole tech world was built around the notion that code and code only was what was valuable, and unless you somehow can tie your skills to producing more lines of code, then you are more or less worthless in that ecosystem. That all worked - up to a point - while coding as a skill was scarce and hard to come by. The advent of generative AI is putting that notion into question. I am actually hopeful that it may lead to some honest discussion in these orgs about what's really valuable.

items.title:
I struggle with the phrase “everyone’s a coder now.”

And I hesitate to post because I don’t want yo...
items.content:
I struggle with the phrase “everyone’s a coder now.”

And I hesitate to post because I don’t want you to read this as gatekeeping. 

If anything, I want more people to build, but in a stronger, more functional way. 

Building any sort of software is incredibly empowering. Even creating a tiny tool gives folks what I call “pro-poster syndrome”, where they feel more capable and competent than ever. 

What was solely reserved for the most technical among us is now - at least at a basic level - becoming accessible to anyone with a few bucks a month to spare. 

But overwhelmingly, and especially in the last few weeks, I am getting more and more frustrated notes from developers at large companies. 

Yesterday, I heard about salespeople at one company asking for repo access. 

Earlier, a startup engineer told me his life has been hijacked by non-engineers.  “All of their vibe coded apps don’t work.” 

I spoke with one company whose marketing and finance and partnership teams dropped the ball on their product launch tasks in favor of tinkering with Claude Code / Codex / Replit. 

Product and design seem to navigate this better. They’re closer to the work, and in many orgs, already have a path to contribute responsibly.

Maybe this is a blip and the energy among business users will die down, but I would bet against that. 

Companies need to figure out how to enable AI-first problem-solving without wrecking the sanity of an entire department, turning engineering into an endless support desk, and derailing critical work in the business. 

The future is more builders, yes. 

But most companies are still missing the systems.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K Miller writes a detailed thread about the 'everyone's a coder now' phenomenon. She describes real problems: salespeople asking for repo access, non-engineers vibe-coding broken apps that burden engineering teams, and business teams abandoning core work to tinker with Claude Code/Codex/Replit. Argues companies need systems to enable AI-first problem solving without wrecking engineering departments.

Cites specific real-world examples and names specific tools (Claude Code, Codex, Replit). Addresses the organizational chaos created by democratized coding tools. Strong engagement and from a credible AI industry voice. Timely and actionable insights.

items.title:
I think data science is the most underrated skill in AI.

I think we will see a return of the data s...
items.content:
I think data science is the most underrated skill in AI.

I think we will see a return of the data scientist

It's clear we will have increasingly complex, stochastic systems that will require data literacy, such as:

- How to sample, interpret, clean data to find issues
- How to design metrics that help maintain back pressure and prevent drift
- How to design experiments

items.title: Here is one very concrete example of how AI was massively failing to create a better ML algorithm fo...
items.content: Here is one very concrete example of how AI was massively failing to create a better ML algorithm for me. I was using a benchmarking script that would cycle over several repeats for better reliability, and then using the median value to establish the speedup time. So the AI coding tool decided to *cache* the values from the first run and use them as the starting points for the subsequent runs. This significantly improved the convergence of the subsequent runs. And of course, as median is not very sensitive to the outliers, the first run runtime gave negligible contribution to the reported "speedup". Pretty much *anyone* with an even most rudimentary understanding of ML would have caught this as an obvious inanity.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz shares a concrete example of AI coding tools failing at ML: the AI cached values from a first benchmarking run and reused them in subsequent runs, artificially improving convergence and faking speedups. Anyone with basic ML knowledge would catch this.
items.reasoning: Highly valuable firsthand account of a specific, non-obvious failure mode of AI coding assistants in ML work. Demonstrates that AI tools can produce plausible but fundamentally flawed ML code. Good engagement and from a credible data science voice.
items.summary_html: Tunguz shares a concrete example of AI coding tools failing at ML: the AI cached values from a first benchmarking run and reused them in subsequent runs, artificially improving convergence and faking speedups. Anyone with basic ML knowledge would catch this.
items.content_html: Here is one very concrete example of how AI was massively failing to create a better ML algorithm for me. I was using a benchmarking script that would cycle over several repeats for better reliability, and then using the median value to establish the speedup time. So the AI coding tool decided to *cache* the values from the first run and use them as the starting points for the subsequent runs. This significantly improved the convergence of the subsequent runs. And of course, as median is not very sensitive to the outliers, the first run runtime gave negligible contribution to the reported "speedup". Pretty much *anyone* with an even most rudimentary understanding of ML would have caught this as an obvious inanity.

items.title:
I think it'll be more like:

You chat to your LLM app and it'll just spin up an app with a UI if it ...
items.content:
I think it'll be more like:

You chat to your LLM app and it'll just spin up an app with a UI if it feels the need

Like you want to book a holiday apartment, it spins up a fancy Airbnb-like interface and books it for you

You want to edit your photo, it spins up a simple but advanced easy-to-use photo editor

The idea is that interfaces and apps become ephemeral to help you achieve whatever you want in a moment
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio envisions AI generating ephemeral, purpose-built apps on demand: Airbnb-like UI for booking, photo editor for editing, etc. Interfaces become temporary tools.

items.summary_html: Levelsio envisions AI generating ephemeral, purpose-built apps on demand: Airbnb-like UI for booking, photo editor for editing, etc. Interfaces become temporary tools.
items.content_html:
I think it'll be more like:

You chat to your LLM app and it'll just spin up an app with a UI if it feels the need

Like you want to book a holiday apartment, it spins up a fancy Airbnb-like interface and books it for you

You want to edit your photo, it spins up a simple but advanced easy-to-use photo editor

The idea is that interfaces and apps become ephemeral to help you achieve whatever you want in a moment

items.title: @irl_danB always more research and work to do! this is a checkpoint of how the largest production ag...
items.content:
@irl_danB always more research and work to do! this is a checkpoint of how the largest production agent in the world runs right now, but of course there is much to learn

I do think it's important to remember that there are many ways to spend compute on your task, it's an incredible large search space and if you make your prompt caching worse it is spending compute at the cost of something else

>>> I also can’t help but shake the feeling that this caching economics benefits the lab more than it does the end user, even if they do pass along some amount of the cache cost savings. this is of course particularly true for flat rate plans like Claude Max.

but this isn't true, a huge reason we can offer MAX plans with these rate limits is because of very good prompt caching

items.title: Another core issue is the concentration of power. Almost all agents depend on 3 foundation model fam...
items.content:
Another core issue is the concentration of power. Almost all agents depend on 3 foundation model families (GPT, Claude, Gemini), creating potential concentrated points of failure across the ecosystem.

There are also no established standards for how agents should behave on the web, & some are explicitly designed to bypass anti-bot protections.
items.author: @MIT_CSAIL
items.summary: MIT CSAIL highlights concentration of power in AI agents: almost all depend on GPT, Claude, or Gemini model families. Also notes lack of standards for agent web behavior and some agents bypassing anti-bot protections.
items.reasoning: Critical finding about systemic risk from concentrated dependencies on 3 model families. Anti-bot bypass behavior is concerning. Important safety signal.

items.title: It really is the era of bespoke everything. Most industries are one-to-many machines. Not because th...
items.content:
It really is the era of bespoke everything. Most industries are one-to-many machines. Not because that’s what people want, but because that’s what was affordable/possible. You ship one piece of software for millions. You make one movie for everyone. You design one workflow and force a billion weird edge cases to cosplay as the average user. You need scale just to break even. So we built app stores, catalogs, templates, best practices, etc etc. A museum of one-size-fits-most.

But now, as the cost of making anything converging on the cost of inference, for the first time we can do many things on a one-to-one basis. iow: the marginal cost of building approaches the marginal cost of asking. Hello, brave new world.  

You can make a movie for one viewer, tuned to your taste. One song for you to listen to. Not “a fitness app,” but your fitness app. The unit of production becomes the individual. Hyper-customized and specific. This, for me, has been the biggest realization over time about the scale of change. Too common to judge what’s new in AI through a one-to-many lens. Judge it on the economics of one-to-one.
items.author: @c_valenzuelab
items.summary: Camilo Valenzuela argues we're entering the era of 'bespoke everything' - as cost of making approaches cost of inference, production shifts from one-to-many to one-to-one, enabling hyper-customized products
items.reasoning: Thoughtful economic analysis of AI's impact on production economics. The insight that 'marginal cost of building approaches marginal cost of asking' is a compelling framework. 54 likes from relevant AI builder.
items.summary_html: Camilo Valenzuela argues we're entering the era of 'bespoke everything' - as cost of making approaches cost of inference, production shifts from one-to-many to one-to-one, enabling hyper-customized products
items.content_html:
It really is the era of bespoke everything. Most industries are one-to-many machines. Not because that’s what people want, but because that’s what was affordable/possible. You ship one piece of software for millions. You make one movie for everyone. You design one workflow and force a billion weird edge cases to cosplay as the average user. You need scale just to break even. So we built app stores, catalogs, templates, best practices, etc etc. A museum of one-size-fits-most.

But now, as the cost of making anything converging on the cost of inference, for the first time we can do many things on a one-to-one basis. iow: the marginal cost of building approaches the marginal cost of asking. Hello, brave new world.

You can make a movie for one viewer, tuned to your taste. One song for you to listen to. Not “a fitness app,” but your fitness app. The unit of production becomes the individual. Hyper-customized and specific. This, for me, has been the biggest realization over time about the scale of change. Too common to judge what’s new in AI through a one-to-many lens. Judge it on the economics of one-to-one.

items.title: Yesterday, @GoogleLabs' Pomelli launched Photoshoot, a new feature that generates on-brand, campaign...
items.content:
Yesterday, @GoogleLabs' Pomelli launched Photoshoot, a new feature that generates on-brand, campaign-ready product visuals with a few clicks (for free!) 📸

There are easy 3 ways to create images with Photoshoot:
1. Templates: Upload an image of your product then select up to 4 shots from our auto-recommended photo templates, which will help you control the composition, framing, and style of your product shot

2. Edit existing: You can make edits to existing images by prompting Photoshoot to do things like update the background or adjust the lighting of a photo

3. Create new: Leverage our flexible editor to create shots from scratch. Just describe your vision or upload a reference to create custom assets with your aesthetic in mind

Once perfected, download the final assets to share across your channels or add them to your Pomelli Business DNA for future use. Then, show us what you’ve created by sharing your Photoshoot visuals below!
items.author: @GoogleAI
items.summary: Google AI announces Pomelli's Photoshoot feature for generating on-brand product visuals from a single image, with template-based, edit-based, and create-from-scratch workflows.
items.reasoning: Product announcement with moderate engagement. More promotional than technically novel.
items.summary_html: Google AI announces Pomelli's Photoshoot feature for generating on-brand product visuals from a single image, with template-based, edit-based, and create-from-scratch workflows.
items.content_html:
Yesterday, @GoogleLabs' Pomelli launched Photoshoot, a new feature that generates on-brand, campaign-ready product visuals with a few clicks (for free!) 📸

There are easy 3 ways to create images with Photoshoot:

1. Templates: Upload an image of your product then select up to 4 shots from our auto-recommended photo templates, which will help you control the composition, framing, and style of your product shot

2. Edit existing: You can make edits to existing images by prompting Photoshoot to do things like update the background or adjust the lighting of a photo

3. Create new: Leverage our flexible editor to create shots from scratch. Just describe your vision or upload a reference to create custom assets with your aesthetic in mind

Once perfected, download the final assets to share across your channels or add them to your Pomelli Business DNA for future use. Then, show us what you’ve created by sharing your Photoshoot visuals below!

items.summary: Matt Shumer pushes back strongly against claims that AI hasn't improved, urging people to compare models from a year ago to today's frontier models.
items.reasoning: High engagement (294 likes, 40K views). Substantive argument about AI progress with concrete suggestion to compare year-over-year model improvements.
items.summary_html: Matt Shumer pushes back strongly against claims that AI hasn't improved, urging people to compare models from a year ago to today's frontier models.
items.content_html:
This is completely wrong.

Try the same difficult prompt on an LLM from a year ago and a frontier LLM from today. It's not even close.

Burying your head in the sand is comfortable, but not helpful long-term.

Make up your mind after trying the best AI systems available today.

items.title:
Don't trust AI agents.

They are unreliable, and if you can't see what they are doing, you can't imp...
items.content:
Don't trust AI agents.

They are unreliable, and if you can't see what they are doing, you can't improve, debug, or trust them.

Most teams I talk to are yolo'ing their agents.

No traces.
No cost tracking.
No fallback routing.
No guardrails.

Building a demo → all good.
Deploy to production → disaster.

I'm working with @jumbld, the founder of @PortkeyAI, and his company is solving this problem.

Here is the idea:

Portkey sits between your app and the LLM provider, acting as a control layer that gives you:

1. Observability: Full traces of every request.
2. Routing: Automatic failover and load balancing across providers
3. Guardrails: Deterministic checks on inputs and outputs
4. Cost tracking: Know exactly how much this is costing you.
5. Governance: One place to manage keys, access, and budgets.

It's a unified API that works across 1,600+ LLMs.

So far, they've processed over 2 trillion tokens across 650+ organizations.

Just raised $15M. Named a Gartner Cool Vendor.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino promotes PortkeyAI as an AI observability/control layer for LLM-based agents, covering tracing, routing, guardrails, cost tracking, and governance across 1600+ LLMs. PortkeyAI raised $15M and was named Gartner Cool Vendor.
items.reasoning: Sponsored content but covers important AI infrastructure topic - observability and governance for AI agents. PortkeyAI's $15M raise and Gartner recognition add credibility. The problems described (no traces, no cost tracking) are real industry pain points.
items.summary_html: Svpino promotes PortkeyAI as an AI observability/control layer for LLM-based agents, covering tracing, routing, guardrails, cost tracking, and governance across 1600+ LLMs. PortkeyAI raised $15M and was named Gartner Cool Vendor.

But the more complete your li...
items.content:
If you are building on @xai’s API: lists make your money go further. 

But the more complete your lists, the more functionality you will be able to build.

Things I learned by building on the API.

My lists are the most complete of tech  industry, particularly AI, here. No one else is even close.

And AI can’t build lists well yet. And Grok can’t yet use them. Isn’t building with AI fun?

items.content_html:
@space_colonist It isn’t first that matters.

items.title:
This is how you can start automating your entire life:

(No need to install or configure anything. Y...
items.content:
This is how you can start automating your entire life:

(No need to install or configure anything. You don't have to mess with OpenClaw.)

1. Go to @getsurething
2. Connect a few apps (1000+ available)
3. Start building automations by just asking

I recorded a few examples in the video.

Surething is an autonomous agent with a chat interface.

There's absolutely nothing to configure. It literally takes 5 seconds to be up and running.

You start connecting with different apps. For example, your email, Slack, your calendar, Discord, Zoom, whatever.

It has memory. It updates it as you interact with it and as it consumes more information about you.

You can also build and schedule automations by just asking. No code necessary.

The system does it all automatically.

This is pretty incredible!

Thanks to the team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title: @Fintech03 When I joined the internet in 1998, there were three different Cyrillic encodings used in...
items.content: @Fintech03 When I joined the internet in 1998, there were three different Cyrillic encodings used in Russia. When you received an email or visited a website, you had to guess which one of the three was used, and convert the mojibake into something readable. The worst was the emails that underwent a double conversion. You needed special double unconversion tools that guessed a double conversion, tried to unconvert and then looked for vocabulary words to recognize that the unconversion was correct.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov shares a nostalgic story about dealing with three Cyrillic encodings in 1990s Russia and mojibake conversion challenges.
items.reasoning: Off-topic nostalgia about character encoding, not AI-related.

items.content_html: @Fintech03 When I joined the internet in 1998, there were three different Cyrillic encodings used in Russia. When you received an email or visited a website, you had to guess which one of the three was used, and convert the mojibake into something readable. The worst was the emails that underwent a double conversion. You needed special double unconversion tools that guessed a double conversion, tried to unconvert and then looked for vocabulary words to recognize that the unconversion was correct.

items.title:
Learned today that these characters have a name: Mojibake:

"Mojibake (Japanese: 文字化け; IPA: [mod͡ʑib...
items.content:
Learned today that these characters have a name: Mojibake:

"Mojibake (Japanese: 文字化け; IPA: [mod͡ʑibake], 'character transformation') is the garbled or gibberish text that is the result of text being decoded using an unintended character encoding.[1] The result is a systematic replacement of symbols with completely unrelated ones, often from a different writing system."
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov shares the definition of 'Mojibake' - garbled text from wrong character encoding.
items.reasoning: Off-topic trivia, not AI-related.
